coredata.dc:description: Metal-modified carbon dots (M-CDs) are a novel class of nanomaterials, including doping metal ions into carbon dots (CDs) and compounding metal nanoparticles with CDs. Owing to their low-toxicity, excellent physicochemical properties, and good biocompatibility, M-CDs have received extensive attention. In this review, firstly, their synthesis methods are summarized with analysis of their advantages and disadvantages. Secondly, in addition to the biocompatibility of M-CDs, the regulation of the fluorescence properties and good magnetic properties of CDs by metal ions or nanoparticles are emphasized, suggesting that M-CDs need long-wavelength emission and high fluorescence quantum yield to improve fluorescence imaging (FLI) ability, high relaxation rate to improve magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) ability. Thirdly, the applications of M-CDs in cancer diagnosis and treatment are introduced. M-CDs can accurately detect cancer-related markers, break the limitation of single-modality imaging to realize dual/multi-modality imaging, construct cancer-targeted drug/gene delivery systems or as photothermal/photosensitive agents to kill cancer cells. Their dual/multi-modality imaging-guided cancer therapy can as an effective mean of integrating cancer diagnosis and treatment. Finally, their prospects and challenges are presented, for providing new ideas for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

head.ce:title: Metal-modified carbon dots: Synthesis, properties, and applications in cancer diagnosis and treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-modified carbon dots (M-CDs) are a novel class of nanomaterials, including doping metal ions into carbon dots (CDs) and compounding metal nanoparticles with CDs. Owing to their low-toxicity, excellent physicochemical properties, and good biocompatibility, M-CDs have received extensive attention. In this review, firstly, their synthesis methods are summarized with analysis of their advantages and disadvantages. Secondly, in addition to the biocompatibility of M-CDs, the regulation of the fluorescence properties and good magnetic properties of CDs by metal ions or nanoparticles are emphasized, suggesting that M-CDs need long-wavelength emission and high fluorescence quantum yield to improve fluorescence imaging (FLI) ability, high relaxation rate to improve magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) ability. Thirdly, the applications of M-CDs in cancer diagnosis and treatment are introduced. M-CDs can accurately detect cancer-related markers, break the limitation of single-modality imaging to realize dual/multi-modality imaging, construct cancer-targeted drug/gene delivery systems or as photothermal/photosensitive agents to kill cancer cells. Their dual/multi-modality imaging-guided cancer therapy can as an effective mean of integrating cancer diagnosis and treatment. Finally, their prospects and challenges are presented, for providing new ideas for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

The synthetic methods and properties of metal-modified carbon dots and their applications and mechanisms in the integration of cancer diagnosis and treatment are reviewed.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were discovered accidentally in 2004 by Xu et al. during electrophoretic purification of carbon nanotubes [1]. In 2006, Sun et al. first named fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm as “Carbon Dots (CDs)” [2]. CDs are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [3,4]. Their carbon core is usually composed of crystalline sp2 carbon [5], and their surface is enriched with functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups. Owing to their excellent optical property, good biocompatibility, simple and green synthesis methods, stable chemical properties, and easy surface functionalization [6–10], CDs have been widely used in a variety of fields, such as medicine, environmental monitoring, catalyst preparation, energy development, electrochemical analysis [11–20].

It has been reported that CDs with metal elements have special physicochemical properties (e.g., fluorescence and magnetism) after the structures of CDs are regulated by doping or compounding metals, making them potential applications for detection, imaging, and treatment applications. CDs are compounded with molybdenum nanosheets, MnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), and gold nanoparticles (AuNRs) to modulate their fluorescence intensity for biosensing [21–23]. Doping CDs with metals can alter their electron density distributions and energy gaps because metal ions have more electrons, unoccupied orbitals, and larger atomic radii, which induces changes in optical, electronic, and magnetic properties [24]. For example, lanthanide (ytterbium and neodymium) doped CDs exhibit near-infrared (NIR) emission [25], some magnetic metallic elements (gadolinium, manganese [26], iron [27], etc.) doped CDs can be used as advanced probes with fluorescence-magnetic resonance dual imaging (FLI-MRI), while other metal (hafnium [28], barium [29], dysprosium [30], gold [31], etc.) doped CDs are used in fluorescence-computed tomography imaging (FLI-CTI) and fluorescence-photoacoustic imaging (FLI-PAI). In addition to biosensing and imaging, metal-doped CDs are used as carriers to carry chemotherapeutic drugs for precision chemotherapy. Researchers doped transition metals (copper [32], nickel [33], ruthenium [34], etc.) into CDs to broaden the absorption range of CDs, improve their electronic transition ability, and enhancing the generation of oxygen free radicals, making CDs have potentially applications in photodynamic and photothermal therapy. Therefore, metal-doped/compounded CDs (M-CDs) show great promise for applications in the diagnosis and treatment of cancer.

To date, some researchers have reviewed the synthesis, chemical and physical properties, and biological applications of M-CDs. Tejwan et al. described in detail the synthesis of M-CDs and reviewed their applications in areas related to antibacterial, antioxidants, bioimaging agents, and cancer phototherapy [35]. Li et al. summarized the applications of M-CDs in photovoltaics, imaging, phototherapy, and catalysis [36]. However, the effect of metal ions or metal nanoparticles on the physical and chemical properties of CDs and the mechanism of M-CDs in cancer diagnosis and treatment remain to be further studied. Their regular summaries are beneficial for researchers to keep abreast of the latest advances in cancer treatment instantly. Based on summarizing various synthesis methods and their advantages and disadvantages of M-CDs, the application of M-CDs in the integration of cancer diagnosis and treatment are summarized and reviewed (Fig. 1 ) in this paper. Moreover, the regulation of the fluorescence properties (quantum yield (QY), intensity, and emission wavelength) of CDs and the endowment of good magnetic properties by metal ions or metal nanoparticles are emphasized, in which the performance modulation mechanism is analyzed. Furthermore, the mechanisms and applications of M-CDs with the biological and physical-chemical properties in cancer diagnosis and treatment are emphasized, including biosensors, bioimaging, and cancer therapy.

At present, M-CDs are mainly synthesized by doping metal ions, metal oxides, and compounding metal nanoparticles. They generally fall into two categories: one-step and multi-step methods. One-step methods include hydrothermal [33], solvent thermal [37], microwave [38], and pyrolysis [39]. In the multi-step method [40], CDs are first synthesized and then metal ions are doped into the CDs or metal nanoparticles are compounded with CDs to prepare M-CDs (Fig. 2 ).

Hydrothermal method refers to the chemical reaction carried out under the condition of high temperature and high pressure in which water is used as solvent and the raw material is placed in an airtight container. It is the most used synthesis method for M-CDs at present. Compared with other preparation methods, hydrothermal method has the advantages of low cost and simple operation. Molkenova et al. synthesized barium-doped CDs (Ba-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method using dextrose and barium nitrate as raw materials [29]. Li et al. prepared manganese and dysprosium-doped CDs (Mn, Dy-CDs) by one-step hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 6 h using aminophylline hydrate, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dehydrate and citric acid (CA) as the raw material (Fig. 2(a)) [30]Tian et al. prepared nickel-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) using ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), ethylenediamine (EAN), and nickel chloride hexahydrate by hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 6 h (Fig. 2(b)) [33]. Lin et al. prepared copper-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) by one-step hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 12 h using EDTA, copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu (NO3)2·3H2O) as the raw material. From the transmission electron microscope images, it can be seen that the size distribution of Cu-CDs was uniform and well dispersed, and their average diameter was 2.8 nm [42]. Fang et al. synthesized holmium-doped CDs (Ho-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method using CA, branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI), and diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid hydrate holmium (III) dihydrogen salt (Ho-DTPA) as raw materials (Fig. 2(c)) [41].

Solvothermal method is developed based on hydrothermal method, which takes organic solvent as reaction solvent. Compared with the hydrothermal method, solvothermal method may incorporate harmful organic solvents in the reaction process, and residual organic solvents need to be further purified, but it is relatively simple and easy to control. Zhu et al. used 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol and copper chloride dihydrate as raw materials, which were uniformly dissolved in ethanol under ultrasound, and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. The resulting product was purified by silica gel column chromatography, and finally, pure Cu-CDs with double-emission wavelength of 426/488 nm were obtained (Fig. 2(d)) [37]. Gadolinium-doped CDs (Gd-CDs) with excitation-dependent properties were synthesized by solvothermal method (at 200 °C for 4 h) with gadolinium chloride (GdCl3) and polyetherimide (PEI) as raw materials, dimethylformamide as solvent. The obtained Gd-CDs can be used as a fluorescent and colorimetric bimodal sensor for the detection of dopamine hydrochloride and d-glutamic acid in aqueous solutions and biological samples [43].

The microwave method utilizes microwave radiation to rapidly generate high heat, which can make the carbon source dehydrate and carbonize in a relatively short period of time to synthesize M-CDs. Li et al. dissolved CA, GdCl3, and EAN in deionized water, and heated them in a microwave oven at 750 W for 8 min (Fig. 2(e)). Then the crude products were dialyzed and freeze-dried to obtain pure Gd-CDs, which are spherical in shape with a diameter of about 6.42 ± 1.36 nm, emit blue light, and have a QY of 11.7 % [38]. Tammina et al. dissolved CA, EAN, and zinc acetate in distilled water and placed them in a microwave oven at 850 W for 1 min, at which time the temperature reached 150 °C. Followed by a continuous reaction at 700 W for 30 min, the reaction mixture finally were centrifuged and filtered to obtain the zinc-doped CDs (Zn-CDs) [44]. Hasanzadeh et al. prepared five different Zn-CDs (CDa, CDb, CDc, CDd, and CDe) by microwave method using CA, PEI, and different zinc salts (Zinc nitrate, zinc acetate, zinc sulfate, zinc sulfide, and zinc chloride (ZnCl2) as raw materials, respectively [45].

Pyrolysis method is mainly taking organic and metal compounds as raw materials to form M-CDs by pyrolysis and carbonization at high temperatures. Liu et al. obtained water-soluble europium-doped CDs (Eu-CDs) with CA and europium nitrate hexahydrate by oil bath (200 °C, 5 min) [46]. Duan et al. synthesized Cu-CDs by pyrolysis of CA and europium nitrate hexahydrate at 230 °C for 20 min [47]. Wu et al. prepared Cu-CDs with photocatalytic performance by pyrolysis reaction at 250 °C using Na2[Cu (EDTA)] as a raw material. Cu-CDs are uniformly dispersed, and the average size is about 2.3 nm. It was found that Cu-N dopant can enhance the electron accepting, electron donating ability, and conductivity of CDs, which are beneficial to the electron transfer of CDs and could further improve their photocatalytic ability [39]. Su et al. took CA, thiourea, and hafnium chloride as raw materials to synthesize hafnium-doped CDs (Hf-CDs) through pyrolysis (at 180 °C, for 1 h), filtration and dialysis (Fig. 2(f)) [28].

Multi-step method refers to synthesis CDs at first, and then coordinate with metal ions or form chemical bonds with functional groups on metal nanoparticle to prepare M-CDs. Wang et al. used levofloxacin, dilute hydrochloric acid solution, and glucose solution as raw materials by microwave method to obtain fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs (F, N-CDs). Then the F, N-CDs were added to an aqueous solution containing iron ions (Fe3+), and the crude product was obtained after the reaction under ultrasound for 20 min to synthesis stable Fe3+@F, N-CDs, in which Fe3+ and surface groups (e.g., fluorine, tertiary amine, and carboxyl groups) on F, N-CDs had strong coordination interactions [40]. Li et al. first synthesized CDs by pyrolysis of tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane at 230 °C for 20 min. Subsequently, iron-doped CDs (Fe-CDs) were prepared by mixing the CDs solution with ferric chloride solution (volume ratio is 10:1) [48]. Hu et al. prepared sulfur-doped CDs (S-CDs) with abundant sulfonate surface groups by pyrolysis method and using sodium p-styrene sulfonate as the raw material. Under ultrasonication, tin tetrachloride was directly added to the aqueous solution of S-CDs to obtain the complexes of tin ions (Sn4+) and S-CDs (Sn@S-CDs). The Sn4+@S-CDs solution was vaporized and dried to obtain the complex of Sn nanocluster powder (named Sn nanocluster@CDs), which has stronger fluorescence and higher 1O2 yield than S-CDs (Fig. 2(g)) [49]. In addition, Fahmi et al. synthesized CDs conjugated nanoparticles by a multistep method. Firstly, manganese (II) acetylacetonate and iron (III) acetylacetonate were dissolved in benzyl ether and oleylamine by heating to obtain oil-soluble MNPs, Then, the water-soluble MNPs formed by ligand exchange were mixed with ethanol solution of 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CBBA) to prepare CBBA-MNPs. Finally, the CDs prepared by pyrolysis of CA were ultrasonically mixed with the CBBA-MNPs and then dialyzed to obtain CDs@CBBA-MNPs [22].

In summary, compared with the multi-step method, the one-step process is simple and easy to operate, in which the hydrothermal/solvothermal/pyrolysis methods are widely used owing to their advantages of simplicity, low cost, and environmental protection. However, these methods are more time-consuming, and the reaction require several hours (usually 12−48 h) to complete. The microwave method can rapidly synthesize M-CDs to greatly improve their production yield, in which the carbon source can be dehydrated and carbonized to form M-CDs in a few minutes. However, it is difficult to control the size of M-CDs by this method. Although the multi-step method is relatively complex, it can effectively regulate the surface structure of M-CDs during preparation process to achieve the expected results. Each synthesis strategy has advantages and disadvantages and can be selected based on different applications (Table 1 ).

Since CDs have been synthesized, a large number of researches on modulating their structures and properties have been emerged, aiming to improve the luminescence efficiency of CDs, modulate their emission wavelength, and broaden their applications [57,58]. Among them, M-CDs not only possess the basic properties of CDs such as excellent biocompatibility, but also metal can effectively enhance the fluorescence intensity and QY of CDs, modulate their emission wavelengths, and endow them with fluorescence, magnetic, X-ray, acoustic properties, which will improve their sensing ability for cancer-related markers such as glycoproteins, glutathione and promote their integration in dual/multi-modality imaging and therapy of cancer.

The evaluation of cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of M-CDs is essential for their biological applications. The main evaluation methods include the 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay [59], cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay [27], hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining [60], and serologic testing [61].

Xu et al. investigated the cell viability of the human hepatocellular carcinoma cell line HepG2 cells co-incubated with Gd-CDs using the CCK-8 assay, and results showed that the cell survival rate is about 96 % (Fig. 3 (a)). After the mice were injected with Gd-CDs for one week, the organs were collected and stained with H&E staining method, and the results of histological evaluation showed that Gd-CDs do not cause obvious tissue damage, meanwhile the body weight change trend of mice in the experimental group and the control group was basically the same (Fig. 3(b)), which indicated that Gd-CDs have good biocompatibility [62]. Das et al. investigated the biocompatibility of Mn-CDs using the MTT assay. Results showed that even when the cells were incubated in Mn-CDs at a concentration of 4 mg mL−1, the survival rate of the cells was still greater than 80 % (p ≤ 0.05), which demonstrated that Mn-CDs exhibited excellent cytocompatibility. After intravenous injection of Mn-CDs into mice, MRI showed that the main metabolic pathway of Mn-CDs was through the kidney, and histology confirmed that there were no special traces of toxicity in kidney, demonstrating that Mn-CDs have almost negligible toxicity and good biocompatibility [63].

The cytotoxicity of Fe-CDs was evaluated by Huang et al. by CCK-8 assay experiments on lung cancer human alveolar basal epithelial cells (A549 cells). Results showed that cell viability was greater than 90 % even when cell cultured in the Fe-CDs solution at concentration of 250 μg mL−1 for 24 h. The in vivo experiments suggested that after 4 weeks of Fe-CDs injection the body weight changes of mice were almost the same as those of the untreated group, and after the injection of Fe-CDs into mice for 24 h, no obvious structural or cellular abnormalities or any inflammatory lesions were detected in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney sections of the mice (Fig. 3(c)). These results indicate that Fe-CDs are very safe for in vivo application at current dose levels. In addition, the biodistribution and excretion of Fe-CDs were further investigated, and MRI showed that the signal intensity reached 93.5 % in the kidney of mice injected with Fe-CDs for 5 min, which indicated that Fe-CDs was mainly metabolized through kidneys (Fig. 3(d)). Both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs have low toxicity and could be metabolized efficiently [27]. Therefore, M-CDs with low toxicity, good biocompatibility and metabolizability from the body provide a guarantee for their biological applications.

The fluorescence intensity and QY of M-CDs play important role in biological imaging. The higher they are, the better the imaging quality is. The fluorescence intensity and QY can be improved when CDs are doped with metal ions. The research results of the molecular orbitals of M-CDs, have shown that when CDs are doped with metal ions, their structure is distorted, resulting in a downward motion of the electron wave function distribution, which contributes to the charge transfer from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), thereby enhancing their QY. This is also demonstrated by the electron state density, which showed that there are significant impurity-induced states in the middle of the band gap and closer to the top of the valence band suggesting that the suspended bonds of metal ions can act as an energy gradient in M-CDs to help the efficient expulsion of electrons or holes from the surface of the CDs, and thereby increasing charge transfer and enhancing QY (Fig. 4 (a)) [64,65]. Xu et al. prepared Zn-CDs with blue fluorescence, and the QY could reach 32.3 % with the increase of Zn2+ in the reaction mixture [64].Yu et al. synthesized blue emissive Gd-CDs, and under a UV light (360 nm) excitation, both CDs and Gd-CDs emit bright blue fluorescence (448 nm). The emission wavelength is not changed, and fluorescence intensity is enhanced dramatically after Gd3+ was coordinated to CDs and reaches maximum when the concentration of Gd3+ was 2 at.% (Fig. 4(b)) [66].

The fluorescence mechanism of CDs is often explained by surface states and energy level transition [67]. The doping of metal ions can induce more new energy levels in CDs to generate more diverse surface sites and longer emission wavelengths, which can effectively improve FLI capability. In addition to HOMO and HOMO+1, M-CDs have a new HOMO+2 surface energy level, inducing the reduction of energy gap, and introduction of new electron transition. Meanwhile the excited electrons can be simultaneously deactivated by radiative recombination, which makes the emission wavelength red-shifted (Fig. 4(c)). The M-CDs with long wavelength emission have stronger tissue penetration ability and better imaging ability, which is beneficial to more accurate monitoring efficacy of cancer. Cheng et al. indicated that compared with pure CDs, Zn-CDs have a new HOMO+2 level, resulting in the fluorescence emission wavelength red shifting to the yellow emission region (Fig. 4(d)) [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence combing magnetic, X-ray, acoustic properties
ce:section.ce:para: CDs modified by metal ions or metal nanoparticles are endowed with fluorescence, magnetic, X-ray, and acoustic properties, so that they can be used for dual/multi-modality imaging combining FLI with MRI, CTI, and PAI, and precise diagnosis and treatment of cancer.

When the paramagnetic metal ions, such as Gd3+, Mn2+, and Fe2+, are doped into CDs, which are endowed with MRI imaging ability [68]. In a static magnetic field, the hydrogen nuclei in biological tissues undergo magnetic resonance at a specific frequency of radio frequency pulses. After stopping the radio frequency pulse, the hydrogen nuclei return from the high-energy state to the low-energy state in a relaxed manner, which includes longitudinal and transverse relaxation. The relaxation time is usually represented by T1 and T2, and their reciprocal is called the longitudinal relaxation rate R1 and the transverse relaxation rate R2, respectively. During this relaxation process, an MR signal is produced, which is followed by the reception, spatial encoding, and image reconstruction of signal for MRI. When magnetic M-CDs are used for MRI in biological tissues, the local magnetic field from the unpaired electron spin around the paramagnetic metal ions can shorten the relaxation time of hydrogen protons in adjacent tissues. Accompanying by the decrease of T1/T2 and the corresponding increase of R1/R2, the higher the MRI signal intensity, the better the imaging effect [69]. The manganese-doped CDs (Mn-CDs) prepared by Ji et al. have a relatively high relaxation rate (R1 = 6.23 m M−1 s−1). Mn-CDs were used in the detection of microgliomas, and the results showed that after the injection of Mn-CDs for 30 min, the magnetic resonance (MR) signal of the microglioma region were significantly enhanced and showed strong fluorescence compared with the normal brain (Fig. 5 (a) and (b)) [52]. The relaxation rate of Gd-CDs prepared by Ren et al. (R1 = 6.4 m M−1 s−1) is higher than that of commercial contrast agent gadolinium meglumine (R1 = 4.7 m M−1 s−1) at the same Gd3+ concentration, and their bright MRI were obtained by scans. The green fluorescence (Ex = 488 nm) in the cytoplasm region of HeLa cells was observed when the concentration of Gd-CDs is 200 μg mL−1 (Fig. 5(c) and (d)) [53]. Doping of magnetic metal ions into CDs endows them magnetic properties, which provides the possibility of achieving diagnostic and therapeutic integration with M-CDs in cancer treatment. In order to achieve good FLI-MRI capability of M-CDs, it is necessary not only to improve FLI and tissue penetration capability, but also to improve MRI capability with a high relaxation rate (R1/R2) [70,71].

In addition, the modification of the CDs by metal elements (such as Hf) with higher atomic number and K-boundary value makes the M-CDs have excellent X-ray attenuation ability. In CTI, the CT value is used to represent the tissue density, which can be regarded as the X-ray amount absorbed by a tissue or material, and the unit is expressed as Hounsfield units (HU). The differences between tissue densities can cause different absorption rates of the beam. When the beam irradiates on different tissues, the different gray values of pixels can be obtained after scanning, and finally the CT value of the tissue can be obtained by calculating gray values. Su et al. synthesized Hf-CDs to study the X-ray attenuation ability of Hf-CDs using iodixanol contrast agent as a control. The results showed that the CT value of Hf-CDs/iodixanol increased linearly with Hf4+/iodine concentration, and the slope of the linear relationship between HU and Hf4+ at different concentrations was higher than that of iodixanol, indicating that Hf-CDs have higher contrast performance and can be used as effective FLI-CTI contrast agents [28]. In addition, some M-CDs have absorption in NIR region. When they are irradiated by pulsed laser, the electrons in M-CDs release energy in the form of light and heat, thus generating pressure waves to form photoacoustic signals. The Mn-CDs prepared by Yang et al. can efficiently convert the NIR spectra into thermal energy. When Mn-CDs were injected into tumor-bearing mice for imaging, obvious fluorescence signal and photoacoustic signal were obtained at the tumor site, and a strong signal was detected after injection for 24 h [50]. Therefore, combining two or more types of mode imaging can not only break through the bottleneck of single mode imaging technology, but also make different imaging modes complement each other [72].

In conclusion, the M-CDs with low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility are able to meet the basic requirements for biological applications. First, M-CDs have good fluorescence properties. Their high fluorescence intensity and QY can improve the signal intensity and sensitivity in the biological imaging, and the long emission wavelength has stronger tissue penetration and better imaging ability. Second, M-CDs have magnetic properties, and their high R1/R2 can enhance the MRI ability. In addition, M-CDs are widely used as drug/gene carriers or photosensitive/photothermal agents for the cancer treatment owing to their rich surface functional groups and easy functionalization. In short, the M-CDs with the excellent optical, magnetic, and drug/gene delivery capabilities are expected to achieve their efficient application in the integration of tumor diagnosis and treatment.

The low biotoxicity, excellent fluorescence stability, unique electronic properties, and abundant functional groups on the surface of M-CDs have laid the foundation for their wide range of applications in biosensing, imaging, and cancer therapy [70,73].

The effectiveness of cancer treatment and the reduction of mortality in cancer patients largely depend on the early diagnosis of relevant cancer indicators (e.g., glycoproteins, and glutathione (GSH)). M-CDs can qualitative or quantitative evaluated cancer-related marker components by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. 6 (a) and (b)) [74–76], electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) (Fig. 6(c)) [77,78], and immunosorbent assay (Fig. 6(d)) [79].

As shown in Fig. 1(a), CDs can act as donors and metal ions or metal nanoparticles can act as acceptors in M-CDs, and FRET is generated when there is a spectral overlap between the donor and the acceptor, causing the fluorescence intensity change of CDs [73]. Alternatively, a possible mechanism in the ECL is that CDs with sp2 carbon structure are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, which may introduce several available defect energy levels in them. It is conducive for the unstably excited CDs to return to the ground state, accompanied by broad emission and stable ECL radiation in the wavelength range of 550−850 nm [80]. Meanwhile, metal nanoparticles with excellent electrical conductivity can accelerate the electron/hole injection rate, which not only significantly enhances the ECL strength, but also makes the ECL onset and peak potentials trend to zero [78,81]. Researchers have utilized these properties of M-CDs to develop a variety of biosensors for early diagnosis of diseases [23,82]. In this section, the application of biosensors constructed by M-CDs for the detection of glycoproteins and GSH is elaborated.

Glycoproteins, as one of the major components of cell membranes, are considered as important recognition molecules during tumorigenesis and progression, such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) [23], α-L-fucosidase (AFU) [54], carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) [55], carbohydrate antigen 15-3 (CA15-3) [83], prostate specific antigen (PSA) [84], and human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER2) [21]. The detection of glycoprotein plays an important role in the early diagnosis of tumors. Shao et al. constructed fluorescence aptamer sensor using the FRET effect between CDs and AuNRs as well as nucleic acid aptamer and DNA hybridization techniques. There is an optimal spectral overlap between donors (CDs) and acceptors (AuNRs), showing maximum FRET efficiency. Generally, the appropriate distance for FRET is between 1 and 10 nm. To reduce the distance between energy donors (CDs) and acceptors (AuNRs), silica (SiO2) wrapped around AuNRs to strengthen the FRET effect. AuNRs@SiO2-aptamer and CDs-DNA probes were prepared by covalent bonding between carboxyl and amino groups, and then aptamers composed of them were formed by DNA hybridization techniques. When CEA was detected with aptamer sensor, it was selectively bound to the aptamer, unspinning the pre-formed DNA double-stranded structure, which led to the separation of CDs-DNA from the AuNRs@SiO2-aptamer surface and fluorescence recovery. Based on this, researchers conducted quantified analysis of CEA and the results indicated that the fluorescence recovery of the sensor is positively correlated with the CEA concentration, and the lower limit of detection value was 0.02 pg mL−1 (Fig. 7 (a) and (b)) [23]. Similarly, Mintz et al. developed fluorescence sensor based on AuNRs@CDs for the quantitative analysis of AFUs in human serum. When CDs and AuNRs are coupled with antibodies, respectively, the distance between CDs and AuNRs is shortened in the presence of antigen (AFU), and fluorescence of CDs ia quenched through energy transfer to the surface plasma bands of AuNRs, so the detection of AFUs can be achieved by recording the change in fluorescence intensity [54]. Yang et al. developed a colorimetric method based on immunoadsorption for CEA determination using iron-nitrogen co-doped CDs (Fe-N-CDs). The lower limit of detection of CEA by this method was 100 pg mL−1, which was 1 % of the classical ELISA method [79]. Nine random serum samples were tested with Au@CDs nano complexes immobilized with horseradish peroxidase-labeled antibody CA 19-9 (CA 19-9 antibody-HRP) based on immunofluorescence sensing assay by Alarfaj et al., and the results showed a linear relationship between increasing fluorescence intensity and concentrations of CA19-9 ranging from 0.01 to 350 U mL−1, and minimum detectable concentration was 0.007 U mL−1 [55].

In addition to fluorescence analysis, ECL is a method that combines the advantages of electrochemistry and luminescence. In recent years, ECL platforms based on M-CDs have been applied for the detection of a variety of biomarkers [85–88]. Gálvez et al. composited molybdenum nanoparticles and CDs to construct an ECL platform for the quantitative detection of biomarker HER2 associated with breast cancer. The method has a low detection limit (1.84 fg mL−1), a wide linear range, and has been successfully applied to the detection of HER2 biomarkers in human serum samples [21]. In addition, Qin et al. established an ECL sensor using graphene oxide-PEI-CDs@AuNRs nano complexes as probes for the determination of CA15-3. The results showed a linear relationship between different concentrations of CA15-3 and ECL intensity was established and the detection limit of CA15-3 was 0.0017 U mL−1, which was lower than that of reported previously (Fig. 7(c) and (d)) [83].

The concentration of GSH in tumor cells is higher than that in the surrounding normal cells [89–91], and the detection of GSH concentration can be used for the evaluation of chemotherapeutic effects and tumor drug resistance in malignant tumors [92,93]. Fluorescence assays based on M-CDs have been used for the detection of GSH owing to their easy operation and high sensitivity.

Shi et al. synthesized AuNPs@CDs, in which the CDs acted as fluorescence reporters and the AuNPs acted as fluorescence quenchers. Different concentrations of GSH were added to the complex solution and UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were measured to investigate the relationship between fluorescence intensity and GSH concentration in a certain range. It was found that their relationship is linear, and the lower detection limit is 50 nM [82]. In addition, the CDs-based Cu2+ detection platform (CDs-Cu2+ complex) was built to detect GSH by taking advantage of the fluorescence quenching ability of Cu2+ on CDs. Wang et al. prepared blue emissive CDs and coordinated with Cu2+ to obtain CDs-Cu2+ complex. The complex was used for the detection of GSH, which can displace Cu2+ from the complex to form a GSH-Cu2+ complex and restore the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 8 ) [94]. Therefore, M-CDs are good biosensors for efficient and accurate detection of tumor markers, which provides new methods and techniques for tumor diagnosis.

FLI has become a powerful clinical diagnostic technique with high sensitivity, non-invasiveness, and real-time monitoring [95]. Doping or compounding metals in CDs can improve the fluorescence QY and intensity of CDs and modulate their emission wavelengths so that they have better luminous efficiency and are easier to penetrate tissues during imaging. MRI and CTI are noninvasive imaging techniques that produce detailed anatomical images in three dimensions. Many metal ions, such as Gd3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ho3+, and Hf4+ have been widely used in MRI or CTI because of their high relaxation rates or computed tomography (CT) values [26–28,41,96–98]. PAI is also an advanced bioimaging technique, in which ultrasonic signals are generated by photothermal expansion of tissue, and collected by the detector to form PAI when a pulsed laser irradiates the tissue [99]. However, in clinical applications, especially in cancer diagnosis and treatment, single-modality imaging has limitations in characterizing biological tissues and cannot fully characterize the features of biological tissues. Dual/multi-modality imaging can merge the advantages of various imaging techniques, and break through the limitations of single-modality imaging, showing the advantage of cross-scale, omni-directional, and visualization characteristics. In this section, the bioimaging mechanisms and applications of M-CDs will be described in terms of single-modality, dual-modality, and multimodality imaging (Fig. 9 ).

FLI with high sensitivity, good repeatability, and rich information, has become one of the fastest developing imaging technologies in recent years [100]. Li et al. used low-toxicity folic acid (FA) to modify cerium-doped CDs (Ce-CDs-FA) for tumor cell recognition, and Ce-CDs-FA have excitation-dependence and low-toxicity. Targeting cancer cell imaging capability was investigated by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The results showed that HeLa cells in the experimental group (incubated with Ce-CDs-FA) exhibited blue, green, and red fluorescence at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 561 nm, which exhibited significant differences in fluorescence intensities with the control group (incubated with Ce-CDs). However, no significant difference in the intracellular internalization of Ce-CDs-FA and Ce-CDs was observed in human normal hepatocyte cell lines, indicating that Ce-CDs-FA has active targeting capability to tumor cells [101]. Chung et al. prepared CDs-iron oxide nanoparticles (CNPCP) for FLI of human brain tumor cells (SF763 cells). Owing to their small size and near-neutral surface charge, they can easily penetrate the cell membrane. CLSM results showed that CNPCP enters the cytoplasm and emits red fluorescence, and the strength of the fluorescence increases with concentration of CNPCP (Fig. 10 ) [102].

It is difficult to collect data quickly and accurately for disease diagnosis by relying on a single imaging modality. For example, MRI, CTI, and PAI have the advantages of high spatial resolution and penetration into deep tissues but have the disadvantage of relatively poor sensitivity. FLI has the ability of high single-cell sensitivity and subcellular high-resolution, but poor spatial resolution and tissue penetration [103,104]. Therefore, dual modalities imaging can be combined synergistically to improve image efficiency. Currently, dual-modality imaging based on M-CDs includes FLI-MRI (Fig. 11 (a)), FLI-CTI (Fig. 11(b)), and FLI-PAI (Fig. 11(c)).

Dual-modality FLI-MRI probes can simultaneously provide high-resolution macroscopic anatomical information and highly sensitive microscopic optical signals which also have the advantages of integration and cross-validation [66,105].

Gadolinium dimeglumine is a commonly used MRI contrast agent in clinical practice because Gd3+ has seven unpaired electrons and possesses unique magnetic properties and excellent contrast efficiency. However, after prolonged of blood circulation, Gd3+ is gradually released from the contrast agent and remains in the body, leading to the occurrence of systemic renal fibrosis and other diseases [96,105–107]. In recent years, researchers have doped Gd3+ into CDs to reduce Gd3+ release without losing MRI relaxation rate and fluorescence intensity [106,108]. Zheng et al. prepared Gd-CDs (QY = 57.78 %, R1 = 6.27 m M−1 s−1, wt.%(Gd) = 12 %) with good biocompatibility. Subsequently, human renal cancer cell (786-O cell) was co-incubated with Gd-CDs and gadopentetic acid (Gd-DTPA) for 4 h, and the results of MRI showed that the images of the Gd-CDs-treated group were much brighter than the Gd-DTPA-treated group. In addition, at the same concentration of Gd3+, the signal intensity in the Gd-CDs group was almost two times higher than that of the Gd-DTPA group, suggesting that Gd-CDs have better contrast ability in MRI cellular imaging. Multi-color emission was observed under CLSM of 786-O cell and human ovarian cancer cells (HO-8910 cell) co-cultured with Gd-CDs. The in vivo MRI were obtained at different time after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA. Both of them revealed bright signal in the target organ kidney after injection for 30 min. For Gd-DTPA injected mice, the MR signal increased to the peak within 30 min and then significantly declined to low signal in 2 h. In contrast, for Gd-CDs group, the MR signal exhibited excellent contrast effect after 30 min and maintained high signal even after 12 h injection. More importantly, the Gd-CDs group exhibited much stronger MR signal than Gd-DTPA group at most of the test period. (Fig. 11(a)), which demonstrated Gd-CDs can be used for dual-modality imaging in vivo [96].

Mn ions (especially Mn2+ and Mn3+) have strong paramagnetic properties because of the presence of many unpaired electrons in 5d orbitals. Mn ions are doped with CDs to form Mn-CDs, which makes CDs have a strong MRI enhancement effect and they can be applied to dual/multi-modal imaging of tumors. Mn-CDs (QY = 13 %) synthesized by Yue et al. could be used for multicolor imaging of cells, and their MRI increased in brightness with increasing concentrations of Mn-CDs, indicating that Mn-CDs can be good MRI contrast agents. After cultured in Mn-CDs solution, zebrafish embryos were illuminated with a light source to emit bright fluorescence [109]. Mn-CDs synthesized by Sun et al. with excitation-dependent orange emission (QY = 32.01 %, R1 = 12.69 m M−1 s−1) can accumulate in the cytoplasm of mouse breast cancer cells (4T1 cells) and the orange fluorescence becomes stronger with the extension of incubation time (2–8 h). In vivo FLI and enhanced MR signal were observed in the tumor sites of hormonal rats injected intratumorally with Mn-CDs [26].

Compared with other metal ions or compounds, iron ions or iron-containing compounds have better biocompatibility and relatively high electron spin with unpaired electrons in the valence layers of Fe2+ and Fe3+. Therefore, a strong localized magnetic field can be generated in the presence of an external magnetic field, which makes iron ions have the ability of MRI [27]. The fluorescence intensity and MR signals of A549 cells incubated with Fe-CDs (QY = 3.80 %, R1 = 3.922 m M−1 s−1, R2 = 4.99 m M−1 s−1) increased with the concentration of Fe-CDs [27]. The cytoplasm of HeLa cells co-incubated with Fe3+@F, N-CDs (QY = 22.98 %, R1 = 5.79 m M−1 s−1) emits green fluorescence. In vivo MRI experiments indicated that the Fe3+@F, N-CDs provided high-resolution MRI pictures of 4T1 tumor bearing mice 15 min after injection [40]. In addition, several other metals have been used as dopants to construct bimodal probes. Fang et al. synthesized Ho-CDs (QY = 8.2 %), which emit blue light and can be used for HeLa cell imaging.

CTI is the most widely used clinical imaging method, which has high tissue resolution, the ability to distinguish small differences in density between tissues, and can directly display internal lesions that cannot be observed by ordinary X-ray [110]. In addition, density values of tissues can be measured to provide additional information for diagnosis. Some researchers are combining the advantages of high spatial resolution and penetration into deep tissues of CTI with the single-cell high sensitivity and subcellular high-resolution capabilities of FLI by CDs to form FLI-CTI dual-modality imaging. Hf-CDs (QY = 18.4 %) prepared by Su et al. are excitation-dependent, and their CT value is 7.21 HU. FLI of HeLa cells cultured with Hf-CDs showed that the cells emit bright fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity is enhanced with time. In vivo imaging of the hormonal mice injected with Hf-CDs showed that strong fluorescence signal and contrast agent signal were detected at the tumor site, however, when iohexol was used for mice imaging, no CT signal were observed at the tumor site (Fig. 11(b)) [28]. Similarly, Molkenova et al. prepared Ba-CDs with excellent excitation-dependent fluorescence properties and high CT values [29].

M-CDs with absorption in the NIR are a good PAI contrast agent. When the organism injected with M-CDs is irradiated with a pulsed laser, the electrons in the M-CDs stored in the organism are in an unstable excited state after absorbing the photons, and the electrons will release energy in the form of light or heat when they transition from a high energy level to a low energy level, which causes thermal expansion at the aggregation site of the M-CDs after the temperature rises, generating pressure wave to form photoacoustic signal, the photoacoustic signal is accepted by the instrument to generate PAI [111,112]. FLI-PAI combines the advantages of high selectivity in optical tissue imaging and deep penetration in ultrasound tissue imaging to obtain high resolution and contrast tissue images, which can directly measure and track tumor growth or metastasis in a variety of cancer [113,114]. Jia et al. prepared AuNRs@SiO2-CDs with a distinct absorption peak at 800 nm. Hormonal mice were selected as the animal model, and FLI and PAI were performed, which showed that the fluorescence intensity of the tumor region increased with time and reached its maximum at 24 h. The PAI showed clear microstructures with high contrast and spatial resolution, and the signal intensity reached its maximum after 24 h (Fig. 11(c)) [31].

In addition to dual-modality imaging, some researchers have also combined MRI, CTI, and FLI to obtain more efficient diagnostic imaging and more comprehensive imaging information [113,115]. Li et al. synthesized blue fluorescence manganese-dysprosium co-doped CDs (Mn, Dy-CDs) (QY = 31.62 %) with high fluorescence stability in different pH, salt concentration, and amino acid solutions. After co-incubation with HeLa cells, the blue fluorescence was mainly distributed in the cytoplasm according to the CLSM images. CT value of Mn, Dy-CDs solution was 47.344 HU, which showed a linear relationship with the concentration of CDs and is significantly higher than that of iobitridol (31.83 HU). Moreover, Mn, Dy-CDs had high longitudinal relaxation rate (R1 = 7.47 m M−1 s−1) and transverse relaxation rate (R2 = 42.686 m M−1 s−1), suggesting that Mn, Dy-CDs can be used as T1 and T2 dual-mode MRI contrast agents [30]. Gadolinium-ytterbium co-doped CDs (Gd, Yb-CDs) with excitation-dependent synthesized by Zhao et al. (QY = 16.84, R1 = 6.65 m M−1 s−1), and they exhibit blue fluorescence under 365 nm light irradiation. Blue fluorescence was observed when Gd, Yb-CDs were co-incubated with HeLa cells (Fig. 12 (a)). The CT value of Gd, Yb-CD solution was 45.42 HU and there was a linear relationship between the CT value of Gd, Yb-CDs and the concentration of Yb3+. MRI/CTI was performed after injected Gd, Yb-CDs into the mice through the tail vein, and both the MR signal and CT value of the tumor site was significantly increased (Fig. 12(b) and (c)) [116]. The multi-modality imaging can integrate different types of imaging data with FLI, which will be more conducive to achieving rapid and accurate diagnosis of tumors. It is also of practical significance to develop M-CDs with synergistic multi-modality, which can maximize therapeutic diagnostics reasonably.

The treatment of intermediate and advanced cancer is based on drug chemotherapy, but systemic chemotherapy has numerous side effects [117,118]. In recent years, targeted therapies for cancer based on nanomaterials have been proposed. M-CDs have an important role in cancer therapy due to their physicochemical properties such as unique luminescent properties, easy surface functionalization, easy cellular internalization, and good biocompatibility [119]. Firstly, M-CDs can be used as drug carriers to carry chemotherapeutic drugs and deliver them to the tumor lesions for precision chemotherapy (Fig. 13 (a)). Secondly, Mn-CDs can be as a photosensitizer and photothermal agent, producing heat, 1O2, ·OH, and ·O2 − to kill tumor cells under the excitation of specific wavelengths of light (Fig. 13(b)). Thirdly, researchers have also explored the application of M-CDs in gene therapy, M-CDs can be used as a gene carrier to carry genes and inhibit the expression of target proteins for cancer treatment. This section will systematically introduce the application of M-CDs in chemotherapy and phototherapy.

Chemotherapy is a more commonly used clinical treatment for cancer. Chemical drugs can act on cancer cells and kill them at various stages of the proliferation cycle, even G0 phase cells, and much sensitive to G1 and S phase cells. However, after systemic chemotherapy, the drugs spread to most of the organs and tissues in the whole body along with the blood circulation, which not only lead to reduction in the drugs concentration in the target tissues, but also unavoidably damage the normal cells while killing the cancer cells. M-CDs with abundant surface functional groups can load chemotherapy drugs and targeting molecules to construct drug delivery systems for targeting cancer therapy by π-π stacking effect, electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, which can precisely target cancer cells through passive and active targeting to improve the efficacy of chemical drugs and reduce toxic side effects (Fig. 13(a)).

Fahmi et al. synthesized fluorescence nanomaterials CDs@CBBA-MNPs, and they have passive targeting effects. Doxorubicin (DOX) was then attached to the surface via π-π stacking interactions to obtain DOX@CDs@CBBA-MNPs. The loading amount (LA) and loading efficiency (LE) of DOX are 35 % and 42.5 %, respectively, and the half inhibitory concentration of DOX@CDs@CBBA-MNPs is significantly lower than that of DOX. In addition, the cytotoxicity of DOX@CDs@CBBA-MNPs and free DOX under the same conditions and concentrations (based on the LA of DOX in DOX@CDs@CBBA-MNPs) was investigated by MTT assay. The results showed that DOX@CDs@CBBA-MNPs more effectively reduce cell viability than free DOX [22].

However, it is believed that inter-tumor and intra-tumor variability affects the structure of the neovascular system and the tumor microenvironment, therefore, it is far from enough to rely solely on passive targeting of tumors, and it is necessary to combine active targeting to achieve better therapeutic effects. Owing to the high affinity between FA and overexpressed FA receptors on the surface of cancer cells, researchers grafted FA onto M-CDs to achieve active targeting of cancer cells. Chiu et al. combined Gd-CDs with FA and DOX to synthesize the drug delivery complex (FA@Gd-CDs@DOX), which bind to tumor cells through active targeting and subsequently enters the cell to release DOX and kill tumor cell (Fig. 14 (a)). The drug loading efficiency of FA@Gd-CDs@DOX is approximately 80 %. The release rate is pH dependent. Under acidic (pH = 5.0) conditions, the release rate is about 62 %. The in vitro cytotoxicity of free DOX and FA@Gd-CDs@DOX on HeLa cells were measured by MTT assay to detect the potential intracellular therapeutic effect. The results showed that the cytotoxic effect of FA@Gd-CDs@DOX is significantly higher than that of free DOX (Fig. 14(b)), indicating that FA@Gd-CDs has the potential to induce cancer cell death [120].

In addition, Mg-CDs synthesized by Nasrin et al. were modified using FA and hyaluronic acid (HA) to obtain Mg-CDs-FA-HA, which showed dual active targeting properties. Then, epirubicin (EPI) was loaded into Mg-CDs-FA-HA to form the final complex (Mg-CDs-FA-HA-EPI). In vitro drug release study confirmed that Mg-CDs-FA-HA-EPI had higher release rate under acidic conditions. Then, in vitro cytotoxicity tests were performed on 4T1, human breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells), and chinese hamster ovary epithelial cells (CHO cells) lines to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of Mg-CDs-FA-HA-EPI. The results showed that the mortality rate of CHO cells is higher in the free drug group than that of Mg-CDs-FA-HA-EPI group, and the targeting drug in 4T1 and MCF-7 cells with FA and HA receptors worked well, obtaining much lower cell viability compared with the free drug. In addition, cell death by applying the drug in dually targeting Mg-CDs-FA-HA-EPI was higher than in Mg-CDs-FA-EPI. These results confirmed the excellent performance of the dual-targeting system in inhibiting the growth of cancer cells expressing the desired receptors (Fig. 14(c)–(e)). In vivo studies have confirmed that drug accumulation is higher in tumor tissue compared to other organs. The Mg-CDs-FA-HA-EPI was able to significantly reduce tumor volume in mice with less damage to major organs compared with free EPI, demonstrating that this novel complex can be proposed as a new platform for breast cancer treatment (Fig. 14(f)) [121]. Taking M-CDs as drug carriers can improve therapeutic efficacy by protecting the loaded drug from degradation prolonging circulation time, and by enhancing tumor uptake through enhanced permeability and retention effects and receptor-mediated endocytosis.

Phototherapy is a rapidly developing means of cancer treatment, including photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), which use visible or NIR light to increase local temperature or generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) to kill tumor cells, respectively [122–127]. M-CDs can be used for PTT and PDT owing to their ability to absorb visible and NIR light. When light irradiates M-CDs, electrons are transferred from a high energy state to low energy state through vibrational relaxation, generating heat to kills cancer cells. In addition, the M-CDs can transform from the ground state to the short-lived excited state after absorbing light and enter the relatively more stable triplet excited state through the intersystem crossing. Then M-CDs can return to the ground state by type I or type II photodynamic reactions. In type I reactions, activated M-CDs react with oxygen molecules in tissues to form various ROS such as O2−, ·OH. In type II reactions, energy is transferred directly to molecular ground state oxygen to produce highly reactive singlet oxygen (1O2). 1O2 undergoes oxidative reactions with nearby biomolecules, resulting in cytotoxicity, which in turn kills cancer cells. Therefore, M-CDs can efficiently convert light energy into heat or ROS, killing cancer cells locally (Fig. 13(b)).

PTT mainly induces localized production of sufficient heat of M-CDs to kill tumor cells, which is derived from the absorption of light or energy conversion after the infrared or NIR radiation is used to excite M-CDs [128,129]. Jiao et al. coupled AS1411 aptamers on their surfaces to prepare AS1411-Gd-CDs with better tumor targeting ability and applied them for FLI-MRI-guided PTT (Fig. 15 (a)). The results of photothermal performance of AS1411-Gd-CDs revealed that their temperature depends on concentration and laser power at 808 nm laser irradiation. Moreover, they have constant photothermal conversion efficiency, excellent MRI performance, and biocompatibility. After co-incubated with AS1411-Gd-CDs and treated with laser, 4T1 cells were significantly killed (Fig. 15(b)). The 4T1 hormonal mice were used as study subjects. After injected with different materials (CDs, Gd-CDs, and AS1411-Gd-CDs), and irradiated with laser beam, the thermography was performed to monitor the temperature changes in the tumor region, and the results displayed that the highest temperature was observed in the tumor region of the group injected with AS1411-Gd-CDs (Fig. 15(c)). Maximum tumor temperatures in mice injected with AS1411-Gd-CDs were 27.1 °C, and tumor volumes were significantly suppressed (Fig. 15(d)) by PTT. These demonstrated that AS1411-Gd-CDs can be well used for tumor PTT [130]. Cu-CDs prepared by Najaflu et al. with good photothermal conversion efficiency (39.3 %) under laser irradiation. After co-incubation of 4T1 cells with different concentrations of Cu-CDs, the cells were irradiated with 808 nm laser for 8 min, and the temperature of cells exhibited concentration dependence [32]. Tian et al. designed Ni-CDs with higher photothermal conversion efficiency (about 76.1 %) and evaluated their anti-tumor effects in vitro and in vivo, which showed satisfactory photothermal anti-tumor efficacy [33].

In PDT, M-CDs enriched at tumor tissue sites can convert oxygen molecules around the tissue into ROS for destroying cancer cell by external light energy [96]. Hu et al. used a multi-step method to prepare tin-doped CDs (Sn nanocluster@CDs) with blue fluorescence, which can produce 1O2 under laser irradiation at 400–700 nm for the treatment of tumors (Fig. 16 (a)). When HepG2 cells were co-cultured with Sn nanocluster@CDs and irradiated by laser, the survival rate of HepG2 cells effectively decreased, whereas the control (darkness) of survival rate did not decrease significantly. In vivo experiments, the change of tumor size revealed that Sn nanocluster@CDs have a significant antitumor activity when exposed to light compared with the control group (Fig. 16(b)), indicating potential prospects for PDT application [49].

Cu-CDs were prepared using hydrothermal method by Wang et al., which show good solubility, excitation dependence, and high 1O2 production capacity under 400–700 nm laser irradiation. The phototherapeutic performance of Cu-CDs was investigated with HeLa cells, and the results displayed that the survival rate of cells co-incubated with Cu-CDs was lower in the light than in the dark. Increasing the concentration of Cu-CDs and extension the exposure time to the light can obtain higher mortality rate of the cells, which proved that Cu-CDs have a strong ability to generate 1O2 under photoexcitation and have promising application in the treatment of cancer by PDT [56]. Chu et al. prepared Mn-CDs and investigate their role in chemodynamic therapy (CDT) and PDT combination therapy. It was found that Mn-CDs could provide the higher peroxide enzyme-like activity under hydrogen peroxide condition, which showed a tendency to enhance upon light irradiation. Furthermore, the GSH depletion ability of Mn-CDs was performed, which was conducive to amplify the therapeutic effect of ROS (Fig. 16(c)). In vivo experiments, 4T1 tumor mice were used as models there were large and even ruptured tumors in the control group and the 660 + 808 nm laser irradiation group. However, tumors were reduced in the Mn-CDs group and were almost invisible compared to tumors in the Mn-CDs plus laser irradiation group (Fig. 16(d) and (e)). The proliferation and apoptosis levels of tumor cells were further assessed by Ki67 immunohistochemical staining and tunel staining, respectively. Ki67 is a cell proliferation antigen that indicates the proliferation of cells. The results showed that the proliferation ability of tumor cells was reduced in the Mn-CDs group and the laser irradiation group. The reduction was more obvious in the Mn-CDs plus 660 + 808 nm laser irradiation group, which indicated that Mn-CDs had the ability to inhibit the proliferation of tumor cells. According to the tunel staining results, apoptosis of tumor cells was increased in the Mn-CDs and Mn-CDs plus laser irradiation groups (Fig. 16(f)) [131].Some researchers have also tried PTT and PDT synergistic treatment to improve the efficiency of cancer treatment [132]. Wei et al. combined CDs, SiO2 and titanium oxide (TiO2) to design a multifunctional nanoplatform (CDs@SiO2@TiO2) with core-shell structure, in which CDs@SiO2 as the core of PTT and TiO2 as the core of PDT. Both CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2@TiO2 caused apoptosis after co-incubation with HepG2 cells under laser irradiation, but the latter had lower cell survival [133]. Additionally, Bai et al. combinated CDs with metal-organic framework (MIL-100(Fe)) to achieve efficient PTT-PDT dual-mode therapy in tumor cells [134]. In summary, M-CDs can be used as chemotherapeutic drug carriers, photothermal agents, and photosensitizers for tumor chemotherapy or phototherapy. By combining chemotherapy, PDT, and PTT can also produce the effect of "the whole is greater than the sum of their parts'' in precision medicine.

As a new class of cancer treatment, gene therapy has been attracted widely attention because of its high specificity and low toxicity. However, gene therapy alone still has some problems such as difficult penetration to the cell membrane and easy enzymatic degradation, which limit its treatment efficiency [135]. Therefore, it is usually necessary to select appropriate vectors to carry genes for gene therapy. Xi et al. synthesized Gd-CDs and coupled with green fluorescent protein plasmid (pEGFP-N1) to form a gene-carrying complex. After incubating the complex with HeLa cells for 4 h, pEGFP-N1 was transported to HeLa cells by fluorescence microscopy. In addition, Gd-CDs showed good dual-modality imaging capability for real-time monitoring of gene delivery in vitro and in vivo [136]. Valimukhametova et al. used neodymium-doped CDs (Nd-CDs) to carry epidermal growth factor receptor small interfering RNA (EGFR siRNA) to prepare gene silencing complexes. After co-incubating the complex with HeLa cells for 48 h, western blot results showed that the complex significantly inhibited the expression of EGFR protein, achieving the purpose of inhibiting tumor [137]. Therefore, M-CDs as a gene carrier can effectively protect the gene from degradation, deliver the gene to the target cells, achieving efficient gene silencing. In addition, M-CDs can be used to trace genes and monitor efficacy through dual/multi-modality imaging.

In conclusion, M-CDs can efficiently and accurately detect cancer-related markers by FRET, ECL and immunosorbent assay. Moreover, the realization of dual/multi-modality imaging break through the limitation of single-modality imaging in bio-imaging. In addition, the surface of M-CDs with abundant surface functional groups, can load with chemotherapeutic drugs and gene to construct targeting cancer delivery system. Some M-CDs also can absorb visible or NIR light to produce heat or ROS that kill cancer cells through vibrational relaxation or photodynamic reaction, to realize the remarkable therapeutic effect. In a word, M-CDs can be used to detect cancer-related markers and realize cancer therapy guided by dual/multi-modality imaging, which will become an effective means of integrated cancer diagnosis and treatment in the future.

To sum up, M-CDs provide a promising platform for biological applications, especially cancer-related diagnosis, and therapy. The excellent properties of M-CDs, such as good biocompatibility, excellent optical property, unique fluorescence sensing, dual/multimodal imaging capabilities, superb loading capacity, and photoconversion, are prerequisites for their use as effective anticancer drugs. In this review, firstly, by summarizing the synthesis methods of M-CDs and their advantages and disadvantages, it is shown that the one-step method is simple and easy to operate. Though the multi-step method is more complicated, the surface structure of M-CDs can be effectively regulated during the preparation process. Among them, hydrothermal/solvothermal/ pyrolysis methods are widely used because of their advantages of simplicity, low cost, and environmental protection, but these methods are time-consuming. The microwave method can be rapidly used to obtain M-CDs with excellent structure in only a few minutes, thus significantly increasing the product yield, but the size of M-CDs control is relatively difficult. Each synthesis method has its advantages and disadvantages and can be selected according to different applications. Secondly, by summarizing the properties of M-CDs, readers can understand that the necessary biocompatibility of M-CDs, the doping or compounding of metals in CDs can improve the fluorescence QY and intensity, regulate emission wavelength of CDs, and endow them with good magnetic properties through the effectively regulate the structure of CDs. Moreover, for M-CDs with good FLI-MRI capabilities, they not only need long wavelength emission and high fluorescence QY to improve FLI and tissue penetration ability, but also need high R1/R2 to improve MRI ability. Finally, by summarizing the application and mechanism of M-CDs in cancer diagnosis and treatment, such as biosensors, bioimaging, and therapy, it is found that M-CDs can achieve dual/multi-modal imaging and synergistic chemotherapy, photothermal therapy, and photodynamic therapy targeting cancer tissues or cells, which will become an effective means of precision cancer diagnosis and treatment in the future.
(1)
Establishment of technology for the large-scale synthesis of M-CDs. Although a good deal of synthesis methods has been developed, methods with eco-friendly and low-cost advantages are still needed. Moreover, although there are many large-scale preparations of gram-level CDs, and their scale still needs to be further expanded.
(2)
Regulation of the luminescence properties of M-CDs. At present, the absorption and emission wavelengths of most synthesized M-CDs are in the blue-green light region, which is susceptible to interference by biological autofluorescence in bioimaging. Although quantum-limited-domain effects, quantum-size effects, surface states, cross-linking-enhanced emission effects, and molecular states have been developed to explain the luminescence mechanism of M-CDs, more guided theories and methods should be established to finely tune the luminescence properties of M-CDs, such as NIR absorption, photoluminescence, and high QY.
(3)
Improvement of the sensitivity of M-CDs based biosensors. Many M-CDs bioanalytical sensor was developed with low sensitivity and cumbersome preparation procedures, which limit their further application. Therefore, designing M-CDs sensing system by selecting raw materials and optimizing procedures could expand their development of biosensors and implement their real-time testing in various systems, such as portable sensor chips, device miniaturization, and microarray technology.
(4)
Exploration M-CDs and gene complexes for gene therapy. Exogenous DNA has negative charge and cannot enter the cell owing to electrostatic repulsion with negatively charged cell membranes, and nuclease or reticuloendothelial system can lead their degradation and digestion. Therefore, it is indispensable to develop efficient and low-toxicity carriers to protect exogenous genes and deliver them to target cells. Currently, positively charged CDs have been successfully demonstrated to be effective for delivering DNA molecules, but they only can be used for FLI, which cannot achieve accurate diagnosis of cancer lesions. However, the delivery of gene by positively charged M-CDs may not only have high transfection efficiency, but also can accurately diagnose lesions from cells to tissues. Therefore, M-CDs can be served as a cornerstone to construct the integration of gene therapy and dual/multimodal imaging monitoring for clinical cancer treatment.

The application of M-CDs in cancer diagnosis and treatment is still in early stages, and thus there is still a vast research area for the further development of M-CDs. Although studies at the in vitro and in vivo levels have confirmed the potential of M-CDs in the biomedical field, they still face challenges from scientific research, preclinical research, and ultimately to clinical applications.
